coredata.dc:description:
The efficacy of electron-hole separation in a single sonosensitizer and the complexities of the tumor microenvironment (TME) present significant challenges to the effectiveness of sonodynamic therapy (SDT). Designing efficient sonosensitizers to enhance electron-hole separation and alleviate TME resistance is crucial yet challenging. Herein, we introduce a novel Z-scheme heterojunctions (HJs) sonosensitizer using Fe-doped carbon dots (CDs) as auxiliary semiconductors to sensitize cubic Cu2O (Fe-CDs@Cu2O) for the first time. Fe-CDs@Cu2O demonstrated enhanced SDT effects due to improved electron-hole separation. Additionally, the introduction of Fe ions in CDs synergistically enhances Fenton-like reactions with Cu ions in Cu2O, resulting in enhanced chemodynamic therapy (CDT) effects. Moreover, Fe-CDs@Cu2O exhibited rapid glutathione (GSH) depletion, effectively mitigating TME resistance. With high rates of 1O2 and
OH generated by Fe-CDs@Cu2O, coupled with strong GSH depletion, single drug injection and ultrasound (US) irradiation effectively eliminate tumors. This innovative heterojunction sonosensitizer offers a promising pathway for clinical anti-tumor treatment.

(c) The rate constant of 1O2 generation in the presence of Fe-CDs@Cu2O, Cu2O, and Fe-CDs at the same concentration (0.25 mg/mL) under US irradiation. (e, f) Valence band XPS spectra of Cu2O and Fe-CDs, respectively. (g, h) Tauc plots of Cu2O and Fe-CDs, respectively. (i) Schematic illustration of the Z-scheme HJs in the Fe-CDs@Cu2O composites.

CDT and GSH depletion property of Fe-CDs@Cu2O. (a-h) Time-dependent
OH generation by Fe-CDs@Cu2O, Cu2O, and Fe-CDs in the presence of H2O2 (0.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and 4.0 mM) at pH 4.5 (a-c) and 6.0 (e-g). (d, h) Comparison of the CDT effects of Fe-CDs@Cu2O, Cu2O, and Fe-CDs at pH 4.5 (d) and 6.5 (h). (n = 3). (i-l) Time-dependent GSH depletion by Fe-CDs@Cu2O (i, j) and Cu2O (k, l) at the same concentration (0.25 mg/mL).

(a) The cytotoxicity of Fe-CDs@Cu2O at varied concentrations against LO2 (b, c) Cell survival rates of 4 T1 cells incubated with Fe-CDs@Cu2O without (b) or with (c) US irradiation. (d, e) Live/dead cell staining and ROS images of 4 T1 cells after different treatments. (n = 5).

In vivo combined SDT and CDT via Fe-CDs@Cu2O. (a) Schematic illustration of combined CDT and SDT treatments on 4 T1-tumor bearing mice via Fe-CDs@Cu2O. (b, c) Ex vivo (b) and In vivo (c) NIR imaging of mice after intravenously injected with ICG-labeled Fe-CDs@Cu2O. (d) Time-dependent NIR fluorescence intensity of ICG-labeled Fe-CDs@Cu2O in the tumor tissue based on the fluorescence imaging results. (e-i) Tumor growth curves (e), mice survival curves (f), body weight curves (g), H&E (h) and TUNEL (i) staining in different groups. (n = 5).
Construction of Fe-CDs@Cu2O Z-scheme heterojunctions for enhancing SDT-CDT combination tumor therapy.

head.ce:title: Tumour microenvironment-responded Fe-doped carbon dots-sensitized cubic Cu2O for Z-scheme heterojunction-enhanced sono-chemodynamic synergistic tumor therapy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The efficacy of electron-hole separation in a single sonosensitizer and the complexities of the tumor microenvironment (TME) present significant challenges to the effectiveness of sonodynamic therapy (SDT). Designing efficient sonosensitizers to enhance electron-hole separation and alleviate TME resistance is crucial yet challenging. Herein, we introduce a novel Z-scheme heterojunctions (HJs) sonosensitizer using Fe-doped carbon dots (CDs) as auxiliary semiconductors to sensitize cubic Cu2O (Fe-CDs@Cu2O) for the first time. Fe-CDs@Cu2O demonstrated enhanced SDT effects due to improved electron-hole separation. Additionally, the introduction of Fe ions in CDs synergistically enhances Fenton-like reactions with Cu ions in Cu2O, resulting in enhanced chemodynamic therapy (CDT) effects. Moreover, Fe-CDs@Cu2O exhibited rapid glutathione (GSH) depletion, effectively mitigating TME resistance. With high rates of 1O2 and
OH generated by Fe-CDs@Cu2O, coupled with strong GSH depletion, single drug injection and ultrasound (US) irradiation effectively eliminate tumors. This innovative heterojunction sonosensitizer offers a promising pathway for clinical anti-tumor treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The escalating prevalence of malignant tumors presents a formidable challenge to global health, necessitating innovative approaches in medical science [1–3]. Within this context, nanomedicine has emerged as a promising avenue for non-invasive tumor treatments due to its precise spatial and temporal control and tumor specificity [4–6]. Phototherapy modalities such as photodynamic therapy (PDT) [7–11] and photothermal therapy (PTT) [12–16] have seen significant advancements. However, their limited tissue penetration restricts their utility in treating deep-seated tumors and broader clinical applications. Ultrasound (US), a mechanical wave with a tissue penetration depth exceeding 10 cm [17,18], has become widely employed in biomedical fields. Therefore, the development of US-induced SDT holds significant promise as a non-invasive treatment strategy to supplant traditional PDT and PTT.

Sonodynamic therapy (SDT) operates on the principle of sonosensitizers generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) under US irradiation [19], effectively exterminating tumor cells. Therefore, the development of high-efficiency sonosensitizers is crucial for advancing SDT. Early investigations focused on organic sonosensitizers [20–22], typically represented by porphyrin derivatives and other lipophilic small molecules. However, these organic sonosensitizers often suffer from high phototoxicity and inadequate tumor targeting, resulting in suboptimal SDT efficiency [23,24]. Recently, inorganic sonosensitizers have been considered a highly promising class of sonosensitizers due to their relatively good stability and physicochemical properties [25–27]. Specifically, inorganic sonosensitizers are not easily destroyed by US, which can improve the stability of sonosensitizers. The toxicity of inorganic sonosensitizers is weak without US, which can reduce the potential immunogenicity. Moreover, inorganic sonosensitizers can enrich at tumor sites by enhancing permeability and retention (EPR) effects, and thus improving the therapeutic effect of SDT [28]. Therefore, focusing on the development of novel and efficient inorganic sonosensitizers promises to propel the clinical advancement of inorganic nanomedicine in anti-tumor therapy.

In addition to the high ROS production rate, the effective regulation of the tumor microenvironment (TME) by sonosensitizers is crucial for improving SDT efficiency. Tumor cells exhibit elevated levels of GSH and H2O2 due to abnormal vascular structure and metabolic processes around the tumor [29–31]. Notably, overexpressed glutathione (GSH) significantly weakens the SDT effect by scavenging ROS, thereby hindering tumor oxidative damage and apoptosis. Therefore, the strong GSH depletion ability is imperative in designing efficient inorganic sonosensitizers. Moreover, harnessing inorganic sonosensitizers to consume and convert overexpressed H2O2 in tumor cells into lethal ROS is pivotal to enhancing ROS generation efficiency. Chemodynamic therapy (CDT) is an effective therapeutic modality to address overexpressed H2O2 [32–34]. CDT catalyzes H2O2 to generate lethal ROS through metal ions-mediated Fenton or Fenton-like reactions, offering an effective tumor treatment approach. Therefore, designing new inorganic sonosensitizers with SDT/CDT effects presents a promising avenue for overcoming TME resistance and enhancing ROS generation rates.

Cubic crystalline Cu2O, characterized by unique physical and chemical properties and a narrow bandgap [35,36], represents a semiconductor with considerable potential as an inorganic sonosensitizer in SDT and CDT. Cu2O’s narrow bandgap facilitates excellent electron-hole separation, leading to effective ROS generation. Additionally, the presence of Cu+/Cu2+ in Cu2O lends it to CDT capability, further enhancing the efficacy of SDT. However, due to the inevitable recombination of electron-hole pairs caused by a single sonosensitizer, the effectiveness of SDT in tumor treatments is limited. Therefore, devising strategies to suppress electron-hole recombination and enhance ROS production is imperative. Recently, heterojunction nanomedicine has emerged as a promising strategy [37–39], offering practical solutions to single sonosensitizer bottlenecks [40]. By engineering heterojunctions (HJs) to regulate the transfer path of electron and hole, the direct recombination of electron-hole pairs can be effectively suppressed, prolonging their lifespans, increasing ROS production, and addressing limited ROS production in a single sonosensitizer. In particular, Z-scheme HJs offer significant advantages over single semiconductors. Under external energy excitation, electrons on lower conduction bands (CB) in Z-scheme HJs combine with holes on lower valence bands (VBs), resulting in higher CB and VB in Z-scheme HJs, thereby endowing Z-scheme HJs with high reduction/oxidation performance [41,42]. Therefore, the construction of bandgap-matched Z-scheme HJs holds promising potential in increasing the effect of SDT in Cu2O-based inorganic sonosensitizers.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel zero-dimensional nanomaterial, have garnered widespread attention [43,44] and are commonly utilized as auxiliary semiconductors in HJs for SDT [45,46]. Due to its unique small size characteristic (<10 nm), CDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility. Additionally, the adjustable size and functional group characteristics exhibited by CDs offer opportunities to regulate the interface structure and optimize the performance of HJs. However, most of the components of CDs comprise only C, N, O, and H elements, and the absence of metal ions in CDs limits their capacity for CDT and modulation of the TME, thereby restricting the therapeutic potential of HJs sonosensitizer. Therefore, endowing CDs with CDT capability and TME modulation holds significant promise for improving the efficacy of HJ sonosensitizers.

In this study, we introduce a novel Z-scheme HJ sonosensitizer by incorporating Fe-doped CDs onto cubic Cu2O (Fe-CDs@Cu2O). The unique feature of the Fe-CDs@Cu2O lies in the formation of Z-scheme HJs, effectively addressing the low electron-hole separation efficiency observed in single sonosensitizers. Compared to previous reports utilizing HJ sonosensitizers with CDs as auxiliary semiconductors [31,47], the Fe-CDs@Cu2O sensitizer effectively overcomes the limitation of CDs lacking CDT capability by introducing Fe ions into CDs, thereby synergistically enhancing CDT-SDT effects in Fe-CDs@Cu2O. Specifically, the rate of 1O2 generation of Fe-CDs@Cu2O under US irradiation exhibits a 3.5-fold increase compared to single Cu2O. Moreover, Fe-CDs@Cu2O demonstrates superior
OH generation compared to Cu2O. Interestingly, Fe-CDs@Cu2O exhibits strong GSH depletion ability by constructing HJs, rapidly eliminating overexpressed GSH (<30 s), alleviating TME resistance, and promoting ROS production. Collectively, the multifunctional properties of the new Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJs sonosensitizer result in promising tumor treatment outcomes, achieving effective tumor elimination through combined SDT-CDT therapy with a single drug injection and single US irradiation. In summary, we present the first utilization of Fe-CDs as efficient auxiliary semiconductors in conjunction with cubic Cu2O to construct a novel Z-scheme HJ sonosensitizer, integrating efficient SDT and CDT effects along with GSH depletion ability. In the future work, we expect that this novel Z-scheme HJs sonosensitizer can also be combined with other emerging cancer therapies, such as ferroptosis [48], cuproptosis [49], and immunotherapy [50], paving the way for cancer therapy with low-side effects and high efficacy.

All chemical reagents were procured from commercial sources and utilized without further purification. CuSO4, NaOH, Sodium citrate, Sodium acetate trihydrate, Ascorbic acid, and Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. EDTA-FeNa, Glutathione (Reduced), and 5,5′-Dithiobis-(2-nitrobenzoic acid) (DTNB) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. PBS buffer solution and acetate were sourced from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China·H2O2 (30 %) was acquired from Yantaiyuandong Fine Chemicals Co., Ltd, Yantai, China. 3,3ʹ,5,5ʹ-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), and 1,3-Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China.

Cu2O was synthesized using the chemical precipitation method [51,52]. In general, 0.26 g of sodium citrate was dissolved in 400 mL of deionized water, followed by the addition of 1 mL of Cu2SO4 solution (1.2 mol/L). After stirring for 5 min, 1 mL of NaOH (4.8 mol/L) solution was added to the above solution. After continuing to stir for 5 min, 1 mL of ascorbic acid solution (1.2 mol/L) was added and the mixture was allowed to stand for 24 h at room temperature. The resulting precipitate was centrifuged, washed with deionized water, and then dried for further use.

Fe-CDs were synthesized via pyrolysis [53–55]. In general, 2 g of EDTA-FeNa was annealed at 350 °C in an N2 atmosphere for 2 h with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. After cooling to room temperature, the product was dispersed in 50 mL of deionized water and sonicated for 30 min. The suspension was then left to stand overnight, followed by centrifugation to remove large particles. The resulting suspension was filtered using a 0.22 μm filter membrane to obtain a clear brown solution of Fe-CDs, which was stored for subsequent use.

Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJs were prepared by mixing 20 mg Cu2O with 10 mL of Fe-CDs solution (2 mg/ml) at a mass ratio of 1:1. The mixture was sonicated for several minutes to ensure uniform dispersion of Cu2O in the aqueous solution. Subsequently, the reaction was performed for 24 h at room temperature under magnetic stirring. The resulting suspension was centrifuged, washed with deionized water, and dried for future use. To explore the optimal loading conditions for Fe-CDs, Fe-CDs@Cu2O with mass ratios of 1:2 and 2:1 were obtained by varying the mass of Fe-CDs in the solution, labeled Fe-CDs@Cu2O-1 and Fe-CDs@Cu2O-2, respectively.

The structural morphologies of the samples were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, MERLIN Compact, Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai F20, USA). The Zeta potentials of the samples were measured using a Malvern Nano ZS90 analyzer. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained using an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected using a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). Ultraviolet (UV)-visible spectra were recorded using a UV2600i spectrometer. Inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) was performed using PerkinElmer NexION 300X and Thermo Scientific iCAP PRO.

DPBF was used as a probe to assess the ability of the samples to produce 1O2 under the US. In general, DPBF (20 µL) was added to solutions of Fe-CDs, Cu2O, and Fe-CDs@Cu2O (0.25 mg/mL). Changes in the absorption peak of DPBF at ∼418 nm before and after US irradiation (50 kHz, 3.0 W/cm2) were recorded to determine the rate of 1O2 production.

TMB was used as a probe to evaluate the ability of Fe-CDs, Cu2O, and Fe-CDs@Cu2O to produce
OH. In general, TMB (20 µL) was added to solutions of Fe-CDs, Cu2O, and Fe-CDs@Cu2O (0.25 mg/mL). The mixture was incubated with varying concentrations of H2O2 (0.0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, and 4.0 mM) in NaAc-HAc buffer (pH 4.5, 6.0, or 7.2 UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded after incubation to measure the TMB absorbance at ∼652 nm.

In general, the GSH depletion properties of Fe-CDs@Cu2O were evaluated by mixing 1 mM GSH with Fe-CDs, Cu2O, and Fe-CDs@Cu2O solutions (0.25 mg/mL) in PBS buffer. The mixture was subsequently mixed with DTNB-containing PBS solution at different time points. Absorbance at ∼412 nm was measured to determine GSH depletion.

Human normal liver LO2 cells were seeded in 96-well plates (5 × 103 cells per well) and incubated with Fe-CDs@Cu2O, Fe-CDs, and Cu2O at various concentrations (0–0.25 mg/mL). After incubation for 24 or 48 h, the cytotoxicity was assessed using a standard methyl thiazolyl diphenyl-tetrazolium bromide assay (MTT assay).

Mice breast cancer cells (4 T1) were seeded in 96-well plates (5 × 103 cells per well) and incubated with Fe-CDs@Cu2O, Fe-CDs, and Cu2O at various concentrations (0–0.25 mg/mL). After incubation for 24 or 48 h, the CDT property of the samples was evaluated using a standard MTT assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro combined CDT and SDT
ce:section.ce:para: 4 T1 cells were seeded in 96-well plates and then incubated with Fe-CDs@Cu2O, Fe-CDs, and Cu2O at varying concentrations (0–0.25 µg/mL). After 4 h of incubation, US irradiation was applied for 5 min. Subsequently, 4 T1 cells were further incubated for another 24 or 48 h. Finally, an MTT assay was performed to evaluate the in vitro combined CDT and SDT efficiency.

4 T1 cells were seeded in 6-well plates (2 × 105 cells) and exposed to different treatments, including control, US, Fe-CDs@Cu2O, Cu2O + US, and Fe-CDs@Cu2O + US. After 24 h of incubation, 4 T1 cells were stained with calcein acetoxymethyl ester and propidium iodide (calcein-AM and PI). Fluorescence images of 4 T1 cells were obtained using a fluorescence microscope (Olympus, Japan). US irradiation was conducted for 5 min.

4 T1 cells were seeded in confocal dishes (2 × 105 cells) and exposed to different treatments, including control, US, Fe-CDs@Cu2O, Cu2O + US, and Fe-CDs@Cu2O + US. After 24 h of incubation, 4 T1 cells were stained with dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate and diamidino-phenyl-indole (DCFH-DA and DAPI). Confocal images were obtained using a confocal microscope (Olympus, Japan). US irradiation was applied for 5 min.

Indocyanine green labeled (ICG-labeled) Fe-CDs@Cu2O (0.25 mg/mL) were intravenously injected into the 4 T1-tumor-bearing mice. In vivo Near Infrared (NIR) fluorescence imaging was performed using an IVIS Lumina III in vivo Imaging System (PerkinElmer, USA) at different time points. For the IVIS setup, the position of emission and filter was above the mouse, with a focus height of 12 cm, and the experiment was conducted at room temperature, with an automatic exposure time. Similarly, in vitro imaging experiments were conducted by collecting tumors and organs from mice injected with ICG-labeled Fe-CDs@Cu2O at various time points.

All animals received humane care, and the Animal Ethics Committee of the Shanghai University approved all the animal experiments. All animal experiments were performed under the permission by Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of Shanghai University (SYXK 2019-0020). All mice were housed in an animal facility under constant environmental conditions (room temperature, 22 ± 1 °C, relative humidity, 40–70 % and a 12 h light–dark cycle).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo combined CDT and SDT
ce:section.ce:para: For the in vivo experiments, the experiment begins after one week of animal adaptation. The mice were randomly divided into different groups. Due to the construction of a cancer mouse model, female mice were selected for in vivo experiments. The mice were anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection of pentobarbital sodium (30 mg/kg). Three to five-week-old female Balb/c nude mice were bought from the SLAC (Shanghai, China). For tumor models, 4 T1 cells (1 × 106) were subcutaneously injected into the backside of the nude mice to establish the tumor models. When the tumor volume reached approximately 100 mm3, 4 T1-tumor bearing mice were randomly divided to five groups as followed: (1) control, (2) US, (3) Fe-CDs@Cu2O, (4) Cu2O + US, (5) Fe-CDs@Cu2O + US. The US irradiation was conducted in group 2, 4, and 5 after intravenous injection of Fe-CDs@Cu2O. Mice were measured and recorded for body weight and tumor volume every day and every two days. For histological analysis of tumors, tumors from different groups were collected and subjected to terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase dUTP nick-end labeling (TUNEL) staining and haematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining. For in vivo long-term toxicity assessment, at the end of the treatment periods, the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney) and blood in each group were collected to perform the H&E staining, blood routine and blood chemistry examination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structural characterization of Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJs
ce:section.ce:para: Cu2O was synthesized using a mild chemical precipitation method, where CuSO4 reacted with NaOH to form Cu(OH)2, with the assistance of sodium citrate. Subsequently, ascorbic acid was used to reduce Cu(OH)2 to Cu2O (Fig. S1). Fe-CDs were synthesized through high-temperature pyrolysis. Briefly, EDTA-FeNa was placed in a tube furnace and annealed at 350 ℃ for 2 h. The product was then dispersed in deionized water, and the supernatant was filtered to obtain an aqueous solution of Fe-CDs (Fig. S2). For the formation of Fe-CDs@Cu2O, due to the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of the Fe-CDs, their surface carries negative charges. The Cu2O with positively charged on the surface is dispersed in an aqueous solution of the Fe-CDs, a strong electrostatic interaction is formed between the negatively charged oxygen atoms in the carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of the Fe-CDs and the positively charged copper atoms on the surface of the cubic Cu2O, thereby forming Fe-CDs@Cu2O heterojunctions (Scheme 1 ), which is consistent with the previous report [56–60].

SEM and TEM analysis revealed the microstructure of the synthesized materials. SEM imaging of the synthesized Cu2O revealed a smooth cubic structure with a side length of approximately tens of nanometers (Fig. S3). Cu2O loaded with Fe-CDs maintained a regular cubic structure, but the surface was rougher compared to Cu2O, which could be ascribed to the Fe-CDs loaded onto its surface (Fig. S4). TEM imaging of Cu2O revealed a regular cubic shape (Fig. 1 a), along with a lattice spacing of 0.25 nm of the (111) lattice fringes of Cu2O, as observed under high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) (Fig. 1d). TEM image of Fe-CDs showed that the samples were ultra-small particles, with approximately few nanometers in size (Fig. 1b). The HRTEM image shows a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which can be attributed to the (100) crystal plane of the CDs (Fig. 1e). TEM imaging showed that Cu2O loaded with Fe-CDs maintained their original cubic morphology, but with a rougher surface (Fig. 1c), which is consistent with the SEM findings. Meanwhile, Fe-CDs loading on the Cu2O can be clearly observed from the HRTEM images of Fe-CDs@Cu2O (Fig. S5) and the (111) crystal plane of Cu2O and the (100) crystal plane of CDs are observed in the HRTEM image of Fe-CDs@Cu2O (Fig. 1f), indicating that Fe-CDs are successfully loaded onto the cubic Cu2O. Furthermore, the mapping of Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJs at high magnification were obtained by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) element mapping to characterize the dispersion of the element distribution of Fe-CDs@Cu2O, as shown in Fig. S6, the elements of Cu, O, C, N and Fe are successfully distributed in Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJs. Zeta potential of Cu2O, Fe-CDs, and Fe-CDs@Cu2O were performed using Zeta potential analyzer, with values of 11.2 ± 2.4, −28.6 ± 1.6, and −5.8 ± 0.3 mV (Fig. 1g), respectively. Compared with Cu2O with positive charges, the introduction of Fe-CDs with negative charges into Cu2O results in a reversal from positive to negative charges, which also indicated the successful construction of Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJs. This is consistent with the results reported in relevant literatures [47,61,62].

The changes in the phase structure of Cu2O before and after the loading of Fe-CDs were examined using XRD (Fig. S7). In the XRD spectrum of Cu2O, the obvious diffraction peaks were observed at ∼29.8°, ∼36.6°, ∼42.5°, ∼61.5°, and ∼73.7°, corresponding to the XRD diffraction peaks of the (110), (111), (200), (220), and (311) crystal planes of Cu2O (JCPDS 43-1003), respectively. Notably, other impurity peaks were not observed, indicating good crystallinity and purity of the synthesized Cu2O. For Fe-CDs@Cu2O, the characteristic diffraction peaks of Cu2O were distinct, but no obvious Fe-CDs diffraction peak was observed, which could be attributed to the weak diffraction peak intensity of Fe-CDs. The ICP test was performed to verify the content of copper and iron ions in the Fe-CDs@Cu2O. The results show that the content of copper is about 87 wt%, and the content of iron is about 1.5 wt%, indicating a higher proportion of copper in the cations for the Fe-CDs@Cu2O.

The valence and composition of different samples were analyzed using XPS. All peaks were corrected against a standard carbon peak (284.8 eV). Cu 2p and Fe 2p peaks were observed in Fe-CDs@Cu2O, indicating that Fe-CDs were successfully loaded onto Cu2O. Four characteristic peaks were observed at ∼932.3, ∼934.3, ∼952.2, and ∼953.8 eV of the Cu 2p spectrum for Fe-CDs@Cu2O (Fig. 1h), which corresponds to the characteristic peaks of Cu+ 2p3/2, Cu2+ 2p3/2, Cu+ 2p1/2, and Cu2+ 2p1/2 [63,64]. Additionally, corresponding satellite peaks of Cu+ and Cu2+ were also detected in the Cu 2p spectrum. For Fe 2p spectrum, four characteristic peaks of Fe2+ 2p3/2, Fe3+ 2p3/2, Fe2+ 2p1/2, and Fe3+ 2p1/2 were observed at ∼710.6, ∼712.3, ∼724.0, and ∼726.3 eV, respectively (Fig. 1i) [65–67]. In the C1s spectrum, characteristic peaks of CC/CC, CN, and COH were detected at 284.8, ∼286.0, and ∼288.4 eV, respectively (Fig. S8a) [46]. For N1s, a clear peak was observed at ∼399.6 eV, which could be attributed to the characteristic peak of pyrrole nitrogen in Fe-CDs@Cu2O (Fig. S8b) [31]. For Cu2O, characteristic peaks of Cu+/Cu2+ were also observed in the typical XPS spectrum of Cu 2p (Fig. S9a). Two characteristic peaks were observed at ∼530.2 and ∼531.6 eV in the O 1s spectrum, which can represent lattice oxygen and adsorbed oxygen in Cu2O (Fig. S9b) [68]. For Fe-CDs, similar characteristic peaks to C, N, and Fe of Fe-CDs@Cu2O are also observed in the XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, and Fe 2p (Fig. S10).

Having successfully constructed Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJs, we evaluated their enhanced sonodynamic property by examining the rate of 1O2 generation under US radiation (50 kHz, 3.0 W cm−2) using DPBF as a molecular probe. Considering that the stability and nanotoxicity size risk for sonosensitizers in aqueous solution is critical for SDT, the digital photographs of different samples (Cu2O, Fe-CDs, and Fe-CDs@Cu2O) were obtained after storing for 0, 1 and 3 days in aqueous solutions. As presented in Fig. S11, no significant change of the Cu2O, Fe-CDs, and Fe-CDs@Cu2O aqueous solution, suggesting that the size of Cu2O, Fe-CDs, and Fe-CDs@Cu2O exhibited insignificant changes after long-term storage. Therefore, there is no obvious size risk of nanotoxicity for Fe-CDs@Cu2O. The influence of different loading amounts of Fe-CDs on the sonodynamic property of heterojunctions was investigated (Fig. S12). For Fe-CDs@Cu2O, the optical absorption peak attenuation trend of DPBF at ∼418 nm is highest compared to Fe-CDs@Cu2O-1 and Fe-CDs@Cu2O-2 with the extension of US irradiation time (0–5 min), indicating that Fe-CDs@Cu2O has the best sonodynamic property. Next, we select the Fe-CDs@Cu2O prepared under the modest condition (Mass ratio = 1:1) for further other performance tests. As shown in Fig. 2 a-d, Fe-CDs@Cu2O shows the strongest generation rate of 1O2 compared to Cu2O and Fe-CDs at the same conditions. Specifically, the generation rate of 1O2 of Fe-CDs@Cu2O is 0.14 min−1, which is 3.5 times than that of single Cu2O (0.04 min−1) (Fig. 2d). The single Fe-CDs only shows a very weak generation ability of 1O2. The excellent 1O2 generation rate of Fe-CDs@Cu2O indicates that the construction of heterojunctions can greatly improve the SDT performance compared to Cu2O and Fe-CDs. Considering that the holes generated under US generally have oxidizing properties, we further investigated whether Fe-CDs@Cu2O heterojunctions possess the ability to consume GSH. Fig. S13 reveals that the characteristic absorption peak of GSH significantly decreased with the extended US time, suggesting that GSH could be acted as a hole-scavenger in the SDT process.

Next, the mechanism of enhanced SDT property for Fe-CDs@Cu2O was explored by analyzing the bandgap structures of Cu2O and Fe-CDs. The valence band (VB) potentials of Cu2O and Fe-CDs are determined by XPS-VB spectroscopy, with values of ∼0.40 and ∼1.75 eV, respectively (Fig. 2e, f). Then, the E VB of the corresponding standard hydrogen electrode (EVB, NHE) is calculated according to the following formula: E VB, NHE = φ + E VB, XPS − 4.44, where φ was the work function of the instrument (∼4.59 eV) [69]. The E VB, NHE of Cu2O and Fe-CDs are calculated to be ∼0.55 and ∼1.90 eV, respectively. The bandgaps of Cu2O and Fe-CDs are obtained by Tauc plots. Firstly, the UV–visible spectra of Cu2O and Fe-CDs are analyzed separately. The absorption peak of Cu2O is displayed at ∼506 nm (Fig. S14), and the absorption peak of Fe-CDs appears at ∼319 nm (Fig. S15). The bandgaps of Cu2O and Fe-CDs determined by Tauc plots derived from UV–visible spectra are ∼1.47 and ∼2.48 eV, respectively (Fig. 2g, h). According to E VB = E CB + E BG, the conduction bands (CBs) of Cu2O and Fe-CDs are ∼−0.92 and ∼0.58 eV, respectively. Based on the above measurement results, energy band diagrams of Cu2O and Fe-CDs are plotted (Fig. 2i). The heterojunctions are formed by direct contact between Cu2O with positive charges and Fe-CDs with negative charges (Fig. 1g), resulting in an internal electric field. The formation of the built-in electric field can selectively promote the transfer of electrons from Fe-CDs to Cu2O (Z-scheme heterojunctions). Meanwhile, the electrons transfer in the opposite direction in heterojunctions is suppressed (Type II heterojunctions) (Fig. S16). The construction of Z-scheme heterojunctions in this study is consistent with the description in relevant literatures [39,47,70,71]. Due to the migration of electrons from the CBs of Fe-CDs to the VBs of Cu2O, this process inhibits electron-hole recombination in Cu2O, resulting in the production of more 1O2 near the CBs of Cu2O. Therefore, the enhanced SDT performance of Fe-CDs@Cu2O can be attributed to the efficient electron-hole separation efficiency of heterojunctions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDT and GSH depletion property of Fe-CDs@Cu2O
ce:section.ce:para:
Considering that Cu+ and Fe2+ are present in Fe-CDs@Cu2O, the CDT property of Fe-CDs@Cu2O was evaluated under acidic TME (pH 4.5 or 6.0) and normal physiological conditions (pH 7.2). TMB was used as a probe to detect the generation ability of
OH by observing the changes of absorbances at ∼652 nm. At low pH (4.5), the absorption peak of TMB at ∼652 nm increases with increasing H2O2 concentration in the three groups of samples (Fe-CDs@Cu2O, Cu2O, and Fe-CDs) (Fig. 3 a-c), indicating their good Fenton-like catalytic activity in low acidic media. Among them, the Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJs have the best generation rate of
OH. Specifically, the value of Fe-CDs@Cu2O is 0.85 min−1 (Fig. 3d), which is higher than that of Cu2O (0.10 min−1) and Fe-CDs (0.13 min−1). The enhanced CDT property can be attributed to the synergistic effect between Cu+ and Fe2+. At pH 6.0, the CDT property of Fe-CDs@Cu2O is also investigated (Fig. 3e-g). The generation rate of
OH for Fe-CDs@Cu2O remains the highest (0.05 min−1) compared to Cu2O (0.01 min−1) and Fe-CDs (0.03 min−1) (Fig. 3h). However, compared to pH 4.5, the generation rate of
OH declines for Fe-CDs@Cu2O (pH 6.5), indicating the pH-dependent CDT effect. Importantly, at pH 7.2, the
OH is not observed for Fe-CDs@Cu2O, Cu2O, and Fe-CDs (Fig. S17), indicating that pH-dependent CDT is unlikely to exhibit toxicity to normal cells. The excellent
OH generation rate indicates that the construction of Fe-CDs@Cu2O heterojunctions can greatly improve the CDT property. Meanwhile, the XPS spectra of Fe-CDs@Cu2O after reaction with H2O2 are analyzed to further confirm CDT performance. Compared to the Cu 2p spectrum of the Fe-CDs@Cu2O before H2O2 treatment, only the characteristic peak of Cu+ ions are displayed in the Cu 2p spectrum after H2O2 treatment (Fig. S18). This can be attributed to the reaction between the Fe-CDs@Cu2O and H2O2 and the surface components begin to dissociate. During this process, H2O2 and Cu+ undergo a catalytic reaction to produce
OH and Cu2+ ions (Eq. (1)), and Cu2+ ions dissociate and enter the acidic solution. Therefore, no obvious peak of Cu2+ ions is observed in Fe-CDs@Cu2O. Fe ions in CDs dissociate and undergo Fenton-like reactions with H2O2 in acidic solution, (Eq. (2)), and so no peaks of Fe 2p are observed in Fe-CDs@Cu2O treated with H2O2. The XPS testing indicates that Fe-CDs@Cu2O heterojunctions exhibits CDT properties.
(1) Cu+ + H2O2 + H+ = Cu2+ +
OH + H2
(2) Fe2+ + H2O2 + H+ = Fe3+ +
OH + H2

Due to the presence of high levels of GSH in the TME, the ROS (1O2 and
OH) generated by the SDT-CDT combination are consumed, which undoubtedly reduces the therapeutic effect of SDT-CDT. Considering the presence of Cu2+ and Fe3+ in Fe-CDs@Cu2O, Cu2+/Fe3+-mediated GSH depletion was further explored. DTNB was used as a probe to detect the reaction between Fe-CDs@Cu2O and GSH. As shown in Fig. 3i, j, within a very short period (<2 min), the absorption peak of Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJs at ∼412 nm drops sharply, indicating that Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJs have a strong GSH depletion ability. Similar to Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJs, Cu2O also showed effective GSH depletion ability within a short duration (Fig. 3k, l). However, the GSH depletion ability was weaker than Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJs, which could be attributed to the construction of HJs that accelerate the rate of Fe/Cu ions-mediated redox reactions. For individual Fe-CDs, the characteristic absorption peak of DTNB showed a slight decrease during the short duration (Fig. S19), which could be due to the low Fe3+ content in Fe-CDs. The excellent GSH depletion ability of Fe-CDs@Cu2O significantly overcomes GSH-mediated resistance in TME, enhancing the efficacy of CDT-SDT tumor therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro combined SDT and CDT via Fe-CDs@Cu2O
ce:section.ce:para: Given the excellent ROS generation ability of Fe-CDs@Cu2O, we explored its therapeutic efficacy at the cellular level. The biocompatibility of Fe-CDs@Cu2O, Cu2O, and Fe-CDs was evaluated using the MTT method. As shown in Fig. 4 a, even at a concentration of 0.25 mg/mL for 24/48 h, no significant cytotoxicity on LO2 cells was observed for Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJ, indicating its excellent in vitro biocompatibility. Similarly, Cu2O and Fe-CDs exerted no significant cytotoxicity to LO2 (Fig. S20). However, Fe-CDs@Cu2O induced approximately 50 % death in 4 T1 cells after 48 h at a concentration of 0.25 mg/mL (Fig. 4b), which was significantly higher than Cu2O and Fe-CDs treatments (Fig. S21). This enhanced cytotoxicity may be attributed to Fe-CDs boosting the CDT effect of Cu2O in the presence of endogenous H2O2 in tumor cells. For in vitro SDT evaluation, after US irradiation (Fig. 4c), the survival rate of 4 T1 cells treated with Fe-CDs@Cu2O was only 10 %, significantly lower than that of Cu2O (50 %) and Fe-CDs (72 %) (Fig. S22), underscoring the enhanced SDT-CDT efficacy of Z-scheme HJs.

The inhibitory effect of Fe-CDs@Cu2O on cell growth was further explored using the calcein-AM and PI co-staining assay. As shown in Fig. 4d, no dead 4 T1 cells were observed in the control group and the US group. However, clear red fluorescence signals of PI staining were evident in the Fe-CDs@Cu2O group, Cu2O + US group, and Fe-CDs@Cu2O + US group, with the strongest signal observed in the Fe-CDs@Cu2O + US group, indicating synergistic enhancement between SDT and CDT, effectively producing 1O2 and
OH and consequently inhibiting tumor cell growth.

The levels of ROS in 4 T1 cells were studied using DCFH-DA staining (Fig. 4e). Compared with the control group and US group, the Fe-CDs@Cu2O, Cu2O + US, and Fe-CDs@Cu2O + US groups exhibited clear green fluorescence signals, confirming that lethal ROS can be produced in cells through CDT and SDT effects. It is worth noting that the strongest green fluorescence signal is displayed in the Fe-CDs@Cu2O + US group, this is consistent with the staining results of live/dead cells, indicating that Fe-CDs@Cu2O + US can effectively integrate CDT and SDT effects, achieving efficient anti-tumor treatment effects through enhanced SDT-CDT effect.

Flow cytometry was used to perform corresponding quantitative analysis on different groups. As shown in Fig. S23, compared to the control group, the fluorescence signal of Calcein AM and PI in Fe-CDs@Cu2O + US group is significantly decreased and increased, respectively, demonstrating that the synergistic SDT and CDT via Fe-CDs@Cu2O could effectively produce 1O2 and
OH to induce apoptosis. The ROS detection experiments is also performed by flow cytometry using DCFH-DA as a ROS probe. Fig. S24 exhibits that the fluorescence signal of DCFH-DA in Fe-CDs@Cu2O + US group is much higher than that in the other groups, confirming that ROS could be generated by Fe-CDs@Cu2O through synergistic SDT and CDT. These flow cytometry results are consistent with the confocal imaging results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo combined SDT and CDT via Fe-CDs@Cu2O
ce:section.ce:para: After confirming the excellent anti-tumor effects of Fe-CDs@Cu2O at the cellular level, we further evaluated its therapeutic efficacy in vivo. The time-dependent distribution of Fe-CDs@Cu2O was investigated to understand the distribution of sonosensitizers in mice. Following intravenous injection of ICG-labeled Fe-CDs@Cu2O, a progressively increasing fluorescence signal was observed at the tumor site, peaking at 12 h post-injection (Fig. 5 c, d), indicating excellent tumor targeting ability due to enhanced permeability and retention effects. To further visualize the in vivo biodistribution of Fe-CDs@Cu2O over time, the mice were euthanized after post-injection of 0 h, 6 h, 9 h, 12 h, and 18 h, and the main organs and tumors were collected for ex vivo fluorescence imaging. As shown in Fig. 5b and Fig. S25, after an injection of Fe-CDs@Cu2O, the sonosensitizers were primarily accumulated in the liver and tumors over time, which is consistent with the in vivo fluorescence imaging results, corroborating the favorable tumor-targeting properties of Fe-CDs@Cu2O. Additionally, the biological distribution of Fe-CDs@Cu2O was also measured using ICP-MS. The Cu levels in the main normal organs and tumors of the treated mice were explored at 12 h post-injection. ICP-MS testing revealed that most of Fe-CDs@Cu2O were retained in the tumor tissue (Fig. S26), indicating once again that Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJs have excellent localization ability.

The in vivo anti-tumor therapeutic effect of CDT-SDT combination using Fe-CDs@Cu2O was assessed in 4 T1-tumor-bearing mice. The treatment plan for 4 T1-tumor-bearing mice is shown in Fig. 5a. After an intravenous injection of Fe-CDs@Cu2O, US irradiation was applied to the 4 T1-tumor-bearing mice. Compared to the control group, the Fe-CDs@Cu2O group exhibited partial inhibition of tumor cell growth (with an inhibition rate of ∼30 %) after 14 days of injection of the Fe-CDs@Cu2O without US irradiation, confirming the effectiveness of Cu+/Fe2+-mediated CDT effect. Under US irradiation, approximately 44 % tumor inhibition was detected in the Cu2O + US group, indicating the effective SDT effect of Cu2O. Notably, in the Fe-CDs@Cu2O + US group, after 14 days of injection of Fe-CDs@Cu2O, tumor cells were efficiently eliminated (Fig. 5e), further confirming the significant anti-tumor therapeutic effect of the SDT-CDT combination therapy. Importantly, the survival time of 4 T1-tumor-bearing mice treated with Fe-CDs@Cu2O + US group was significantly prolonged compared to other groups (Fig. 5f), validating that the Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJs have excellent tumor therapeutic effects.

The excellent anti-tumor performance of Fe-CDs@Cu2O was also analyzed through H&E staining, revealing the most severe tumor damage in the Fe-CDs@Cu2O + US group (Fig. 5h). The TUNEL staining of tumor sections was further performed to elucidate the specific mechanism of tumor inhibition. As shown in Fig. 5i, the Fe-CDs@Cu2O + US group similarly shows the most severe tumor damage compared to the other groups, which is consistent with tumor growth data and H&E staining results. Thus, the Z-scheme Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJs sonosensitizer exhibits promising anti-tumor therapeutic effects, including efficient tumor elimination and prolonged mouse survival, which could be attributed to the enhanced SDT effect of Z-scheme HJs, the Cu/Fe ions-mediated TME regulation, and enhanced CDT effect.

Biodistribution analysis of Fe-CDs@Cu2O was performed to investigate its retention time in the body. As presented in Fig. S27, Fe-CDs@Cu2O is primarily accumulated in liver and spleen due to the capture of reticuloendothelial system. After injection of 14 days, no obvious Cu signal was detected in these organs, suggesting that Fe-CDs@Cu2O was eliminated from the mice through the liver and spleen. Meanwhile, the potential long-term toxicity of the Z-scheme Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJs sonosensitizer in antineoplastic therapy was explored using histopathological analysis, blood biochemistry, and routine blood examinations. As shown in Fig. 5g, no significant changes were detected in the body weight of each group of mice during the treatment period, indicating negligible impact of Fe-CDs@Cu2O + US on mice. Additionally, all indicators of blood biochemistry and routine blood measurements in each group were within the normal range (Fig. S28). The H&E staining experiment of the main organs showed no significant histological changes in each group (Fig. S29). These findings thus indicate that the adverse effects of the Z-scheme Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJs on tumor therapy are negligible.

We have for the first time introduced metal-containing carbon dots into cubic Cu2O to construct a novel Z-scheme heterojunction for enhanced SDT-CDT synergistic tumor therapy. The incorporation of Fe-CDs not only enhances the SDT effect but also introduces Fe ions, facilitating Cu+/Fe2+-mediated enhanced CDT effect and Cu2+/Fe3+-mediated TME regulation. Due to the high ROS generation rate and strong GSH depletion ability, the Fe-CDs@Cu2O HJs demonstrate excellent tumor inhibition rates and improved survival rates after single drug injection and single US irradiation, with negligible long-term toxicity to mice. In summary, the concept of using metal-containing CDs as auxiliary semiconductors to enhance HJ sonosensitizer for SDT-CDT synergistic tumor therapy holds promise for inspiring the development of advanced nanocomposite materials in the biomedical field.
